text: The patient is positioned supine with legs raised in orthotomy. Surgical ports are placed according to the displayed diagram. The patient is then positioned into reverse Trendelenburg with the right shoulder tilted down, facilitating the movement of the small bowel and omentum into the right and upper abdomen, exposing the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the groove of the inferior mesenteric artery and makes an incision in the peritoneum at the pelvic brim. Medial to lateral dissection is performed, carefully avoiding the ureter and hypogastric nerves. Hemlock clips are applied to the artery, which is divided near its origin. The inferior mesenteric vein is identified and divided at the pancreas's lower border, preventing traction injury. A window is created over the pancreas, accessing the lesser sac, with swabs placed as landmarks for splenic flexure mobilization. The gastrocolic ligament is separated, and the splenocolic ligament is divided, uniting the lateral mobilization with the pancreatic window. The splenic flexure is fully mobilized by straightening it with counter traction.

The surgeon performs a total mesorectal excision using monopolar hook diathermy. The rectum is visualized as a four-sided box, and the posterior dissection begins above the presacral fascia, extending towards the pelvic floor. Care is taken to avoid injury to the presacral vessels. Once the posterior dissection is maximized, lateral mobilization starts two centimeters above the peritoneal reflection, emphasizing traction and counter traction. The lateral dissection is then united with the anterior dissection. The uterus is elevated using a two O proline stitch.

The surgeon begins the anterior dissection after uniting the two side dissections, using a monopolar hook diathermy for precise tissue separation. The posterior vaginal wall is carefully avoided during dissection. Optimum traction and counter traction are applied to facilitate deeper dissection towards the pelvic floor. The lateral pelvic nerves are visible, and care is taken to avoid injury to these nerves during lateral mobilization.

The surgeon completes the total mesorectal excision (TME) and begins transection of the rectal tube using an Echelon 45 stapler, requiring two firings. The rectum is divided and removed, revealing important pelvic structures. A CDH-XXIX circular stapling device is used to form the anastomosis, with the trocar emerging from the midpoint of the staple line running anterior to posterior. The specimen is extracted through a small midline incision. The bowel orientation and tension are checked before firing the stapler, and the integrity of the anastomosis is verified using a flexible sigmoidoscope with a standard leak test. A loop ileostomy and pelvic drain are inserted for defunctioning.
